title: Alcohol ink marbling on water
subtitle: Dropping solvent inks on a plain water surface to create loose, organic patterns
excerpt: Alcohol inks dropped on water spread in vivid concentric rings and blend where they meet. No size, no mordanting, no alum: the technique requires a non-absorbent substrate and a well-ventilated workspace. A sheet takes under two minutes from first drop to lifted print.
sourceNotes: Alcohol ink marbling on water is a contemporary technique with no pre-1928 source. The chemistry of solvent-based dye inks spreading on a water surface draws on the same surface-tension principles documented in Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894): public domain. Practical method synthesised from widely documented contemporary craft practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A water-surface marbling technique using solvent-based dye inks. No size is required: the inks float on plain water by surface tension. Non-absorbent substrates (Yupo, glossy card) must be used; standard paper absorbs the ink before the pattern transfers.
A synthetic polypropylene paper with a completely non-absorbent surface. Alcohol inks pool and spread on Yupo rather than soaking in, making it the standard substrate for this technique.

The Japanese ink-on-water marbling tradition: sumi ink dropped from a brush onto a plain water surface, spread by blowing. The oldest surviving marbling tradition; alcohol ink marbling produces a visually similar loose ring effect by a faster mechanical process.

content.text: Alcohol inks

are solvent-based dyes that repel water and spread rapidly on contact with a water surface. A single drop expands in concentric rings of colour. A second colour dropped in the centre pushes the first outward; a drop of isopropyl alcohol pushed into wet ink creates a halo of lighter tone. The technique produces loose, fluid patterns that are not replicable in carrageenan or gelatin marbling. Because no size is needed, set-up is fast. The main constraints are ventilation (the alcohol vapour needs to disperse), and substrate: standard paper absorbs the ink before it transfers.
Yupo
or glossy card is required.

attrs.title: Ventilation and skin contact
attrs.body: Alcohol inks contain solvent. Work in a well-ventilated room with a window open. Do not work in a closed space without air circulation. Wear nitrile gloves: alcohol inks stain skin and the solvents absorb through skin with prolonged contact. Keep the tray away from naked flames: isopropyl alcohol is flammable.

items.name: Alcohol inks, 3-4 colours
items.qty: Small dropper bottles, one per colour
items.substitutions: Any brand of alcohol-based dye ink in a dropper bottle. Metallic alcohol inks add a shimmery accent used as a final drop.

items.name: Isopropyl alcohol, 91%
items.qty: Small amount in a dropper bottle
items.substitutions: 70% isopropyl also works but spreads less dramatically.

items.name: Yupo paper or glossy card stock
items.qty: Cut to the size you want
items.substitutions: Glossy photo paper, glossy magazine card, or any smooth-coated non-absorbent surface. Standard alum-mordanted marbling paper does not work: the alum surface is designed for acrylic pigments, not solvent inks.

items.name: Shallow marbling tray, filled with plain tap water
items.substitutions: Any shallow tray wider than your substrate. The water should be 2-3 cm deep.

content.text: The move
Fill the tray with plain tap water to a depth of about 2-3 cm. No size, no preparation. Work at room temperature.

Hold the first ink dropper about 3-5 cm above the water surface and drop a small amount onto the surface. The ink should spread into a circle immediately. If it sinks and colours the water rather than floating, the ink was thinned too much with alcohol, or the ambient temperature is too cold. Alcohol ink marbling works best at 18-22°C.

Drop a second colour into the centre of the first circle. It will push the first colour outward and form its own inner ring. Continue adding colours in the centre to build up concentric rings of colour across the tray surface.

For a push-and-spread effect: drop a small amount of pure isopropyl alcohol into the middle of the pattern. The alcohol has lower surface tension than the ink and pushes the colour outward rapidly, creating a halo of clear or pale colour in the centre. Use sparingly, too much alcohol disintegrates the pattern.

Swirl the surface gently with a stylus if you want to blend the pattern slightly. Alcohol inks on water are more fluid than carrageenan marbling: even one stroke of a stylus transforms the pattern significantly. Work quickly.
Take the
Yupo
or glossy card and lower it face-down onto the water surface. Place it flat in one movement: set the far edge down first, then lower the rest evenly. Leave for 5-15 seconds.

Peel the sheet off the water surface steadily from one edge. The pattern transfers to the non-absorbent face of the substrate. Lay the sheet pattern-side up on a flat surface to dry. Alcohol ink dries in two to five minutes without heat. Do not blot or touch the surface while wet.
After each sheet, skim the remaining ink off the water surface with a strip of newspaper before adding fresh ink for the next sheet. Accumulated ink on the surface gives muddy results.
Comparison with other marbling methods

Alcohol ink marbling produces vivid, loose patterns faster than carrageenan or gelatin work. The lack of size preparation and mordanting means a session can begin in minutes. The patterns cannot be combed into geometric grids: the ink moves too freely and the surface tension is too low for comb work. For geometric patterns, use carrageenan. For
suminagashi
-style concentric rings, alcohol ink gives a bolder, more vivid result; suminagashi gives a quieter, more restrained one. The two methods suit different aesthetics.

items.cause: Ink dropped from too high (breaks the surface tension), or water too cold, or ink bottle nearly empty (last drops are often more concentrated and denser)
items.fix: Drop from 3-5 cm above the surface. Ensure the room is at least 18°C. Use a fresh bottle if the last drops are behaving oddly.

items.problem: Pattern transfers as a patchy, incomplete print
items.cause: Substrate partially absorbent; or lifted too quickly before the ink settled
items.fix: Check the substrate is fully non-absorbent. Leave the sheet on the water for 10-15 seconds before lifting.

items.problem: Colours mixing into brown rather than staying distinct
items.cause: Too many colours dropped in the same area; or isopropyl alcohol used too liberally
items.fix: Work with three colours maximum in close proximity. Use isopropyl alcohol sparingly, in single drops. Each ink drop should have room to spread without immediately colliding with another.

content.text: Where it leads

Alcohol ink marbling on Yupo works as a journal cover, a background for papercutting, or decorative endpaper for a case-bound book. Once fully cured, the dry surface can be painted over with more alcohol ink. It can also be cut and assembled into collage, or used as a background for stamping and hand lettering.
